**Moringa**, also called **drumstick tree** or **zogale**, is a tree widely cultivated for use in cooking.

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

The seed pods of the tree, sometimes called drumsticks, are long and thin with a ridged outer surface and green color.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) The outer skin is tough in fibrous;[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) inside the fleshy pod are several seeds.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) The flavor of the pods is grassy, akin to that of green beans.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) The green leaves are said to taste somewhat peppery.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)

* Assorted edible moringa components

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

The pods can be kept in the fridge for a few weeks or frozen for several months.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) Leaves can be stored fresh or dried for longer-term storage.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)

## Preparation

[edit | edit source]

Moringa leaves should be stripped from their woody stalks before use.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) The young, tender pods can be simply trimmed and cut into pieces if desired.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

The leaves and pods are commonly consumed in several parts of Africa and Asia. Like green beans and asparagus, the pods can be stir-fried, fried, and stewed, as they are in South and Southeast Asia.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) When the pod becomes too fibrous with maturity,[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) the flesh and seeds only are eaten.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) The seeds, when tender, are eaten in Nigeria.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) The leaves can be eaten uncooked or cooked.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) Flowers, too, may be eaten.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)

The seeds may be ground to make so-called ben oil, which can be used in cooking.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)
